paper_title: SAE-GUIDE: Sparse Autoencoder-Guided Uncertainty-aware Information Detection and Enhancement for Multi-Hop Retrieval
email_title: SAE-GUIDE: Sparse Autoencoder-Guided Uncertainty-aware Information Detection and Enhancement for Multi-Hop Retrieval

sections.summary: This paper proposes SAE-GUIDE, a retrieval-triggering framework for multi-hop question answering that uses Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to decompose LLM hidden states into sparse, purportedly interpretable features. The method accumulates feature activations over time to estimate an uncertainty score, and when a threshold is exceeded, it maps top-activated features to targeted retrieval queries (entity, temporal, relation). Experiments on a small synthetic dataset show large reductions in the number of retrieval calls versus Standard RAG and Probing-RAG, with the claim that this is achieved while maintaining comparable answer quality.
sections.strengths:
Technical novelty and innovation
Framing “when and what to retrieve” as a detection problem over SAE-derived latents is an interesting direction that bridges interpretability with adaptive retrieval.
The idea of cumulative activation with decay to capture persistent information needs across reasoning steps is reasonable and could be impactful if validated on real tasks.
The concept of mapping feature activations to query augmentations (feature-to-query mapping) aims at more targeted retrieval than generic similarity search.


Experimental rigor and validation
The method includes ablations (binary probe vs SAE-probe, per-step vs cumulative, with/without mapping), which, in principle, are the right kinds of questions to ask.


Clarity of presentation
The high-level pipeline is easy to follow (SAE training → feature annotation → probe training → uncertainty-gated retrieval), and the algorithm sketch is clear.
The paper provides architectural details for the SAE (expansion factor, top-K) and the probe.


Significance of contributions
If substantiated on standard benchmarks with credible baselines, an interpretable, low-latency trigger that reduces unnecessary retrieval could have practical significance for multi-hop QA and tool-use agents.
sections.weaknesses:
Technical limitations or concerns
The uncertainty estimate is just a learned scalar from cumulative features; calling it “uncertainty-aware” is misleading without calibration or principled uncertainty modeling.
The feature-to-query mapping is underspecified and currently heuristic (simple templated augmentations); it is unclear how reliably features are labeled as entity/temporal/relation, or how robust this mapping is.
SAE training and feature “annotation” methodology lacks detail and rigor; it is unclear how features were identified as corresponding to specific information needs beyond automated correlation.
Combining hard Top-K sparsity with an L1 penalty is unusual and not well justified; and training on pooled multi-layer representations while using a single layer at inference introduces a mismatch.


Experimental gaps or methodological issues
All EM and F1 scores are 0.00 across all methods, rendering the central claim—maintaining accuracy while reducing retrieval—unsupported. When accuracy is flatlined, retrieval-reduction claims are not meaningful.
The IRCoT baseline is broken (0 retrieval calls due to implementation issues), invalidating a key comparison. Similarly, Probing-RAG is not shown to be competitive in any accuracy metric here.
The evaluation uses a small synthetic dataset (800 examples), a simulated LLM wrapper, and reports no results on standard multi-hop QA benchmarks (e.g., HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, MuSiQue), which severely limits generalizability.
The paper lists Retrieval Precision@3 as a metric but does not report it; there is no cost–quality tradeoff analysis (e.g., plots of EM/F1 versus retrieval calls as the threshold varies).
No latency or computational overhead measurements are provided for SAE encoding, cumulative tracking, or mapping, which is important for claims about “real-time” operation.
Feature interpretability is claimed but not demonstrated with human validation or standardized interpretability metrics; the analysis is “automated,” with no quality control.


Clarity or presentation issues
Several figures and tables are placeholders or contain artifacts; some references use future years or placeholders (e.g., “RAGLens Authors, 2026”). This raises concerns about completeness and reproducibility.
The description of Algorithm 2 is referenced but not provided; feature annotation and mapping procedures need more precise definitions.


Missing related work or comparisons
Important baselines such as FLARE, DRAGIN, Self-RAG, and UAR are mentioned but not compared under fair, functioning implementations.
There is little engagement with recent SAE-for-retrieval interpretability/control work on dense encoders beyond brief mention; connections to works like interpretability of sparse retrieval latents and KV compression–based latent reasoning are underdeveloped.
sections.detailed_comments:
Technical soundness evaluation
The central technical premise—hidden states contain latent signals indicating “what” information is missing—is plausible, and SAEs are a reasonable tool for uncovering structure. However, the paper does not causally connect specific features to specific information needs. Without controlled interventions or rigorous correlation analyses tied to ground truth (e.g., bridge entities, temporal constraints), it is difficult to assess whether the method detects the “what” versus generic uncertainty or spurious patterns.
The uncertainty head U_t is a plain MLP on cumulative features. Absent calibration (e.g., ECE, coverage via conformal methods) or a link to predictive uncertainty, the “uncertainty-aware” framing is not substantiated.
The feature-to-query mapping is heuristic and unvalidated; no evidence is shown that mapped queries improve recall/precision for the needed evidence compared to a well-tuned baseline query reformulation.
The SAE training setup (3584→28672, K=32) is standard, but the dataset used for SAE learning is tiny (1,200 hidden states). Feature “semantics” and stability under domain shift are unclear.


Experimental evaluation assessment
The main reported improvements are only on the number of retrieval calls. With all EM/F1 at 0.00, the method’s effect on answer quality is indeterminate. In this setting, one could trivially drive retrieval calls down by raising thresholds—so we need precision/recall curves or Pareto frontiers of EM/F1 vs. retrieval calls across thresholds.
The IRCoT baseline is nonfunctional; this is unacceptable for a core comparison. Probing-RAG, the closest baseline, shows worse retrieval efficiency but no effectiveness results; without accuracy improvements or non-degenerate effectiveness, the claim that SAE-GUIDE is “better” is not supported.
The choice to evaluate with a simulated LLM wrapper and synthetic data precludes strong conclusions about real-world behavior. At minimum, results on HotpotQA (distractor setting) and 2WikiMultiHopQA with a working open-source LLM should be provided.
The paper mentions Retrieval Precision@3 but omits reporting it; we need precision/recall of retrieved documents, hits@k, and grounding faithfulness to establish utility.
No run-time analysis (latency per step, overhead of SAE encoding, additional memory footprint) is provided. Given claims of “real-time” operation and efficiency, these measurements are essential.


Comparison with related work (using the summaries provided)
Relative to latent reasoning works like LaSER (distilling explicit CoT into latent tokens) and KaVa (KV-cache distillation), this paper tackles a different—but adjacent—goal (detecting retrieval needs via interpretable latents rather than compressing reasoning). The paper could better articulate complementarities, e.g., whether SAE features correlate with latent-step saliencies or can serve as triggers for latent reasoning depth.
The retrieval interpretability work inserting k-sparse autoencoders on dense encoders (2411.00786 summary) provides a more rigorous interpretability and control evaluation pipeline (feature labeling, intervention analysis). SAE-GUIDE would benefit from adopting those evaluation standards (e.g., feature labeling quality, intervention success rates) to validate claims about “what” is missing.
Active retrieval baselines like FLARE and DRAGIN (probability- or attention-based triggers) should be included with faithful implementations. UAR’s multi-criterion probes are also closely related and deserve a quantitative comparison.
The distinction drawn with Xin et al. (steering vs. detecting) is conceptually sound, but empirical validation is insufficient to show that detection over SAEs meaningfully improves effectiveness or interpretability in practice.


Discussion of broader impact and significance
If validated at scale, an interpretable, feature-based retrieval trigger could reduce API costs, improve transparency into when models seek external knowledge, and generalize to tool-use decisions. However, the present evidence is not yet convincing. A key risk is overclaiming interpretability when feature semantics are not rigorously established, leading to misplaced trust. Another concern is brittleness across domains/data distributions if feature-to-query mappings are ad hoc.
sections.questions_for_authors:
How are SAE features annotated as “entity,” “temporal,” or “relation”? What procedure, datasets, and inter-annotator reliability (if human) or validation metrics (if automated) support these labels?
How was the retrieval threshold selected? Please provide EM/F1 vs. retrieval-calls curves across thresholds and discuss calibration. What prevents trivial thresholding that collapses retrieval calls without preserving accuracy?
Can you report retrieval effectiveness metrics (e.g., Precision@k, Recall@k, hit rate) and grounding faithfulness for the retrieved documents? The metric is listed but not reported.
What happens on standard multi-hop datasets (HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, MuSiQue) with a real LLM and a functional IRCoT, FLARE, DRAGIN, Probing-RAG, and UAR baselines? Please include effectiveness and efficiency results.
How stable are the identified features across seeds, layers, and domains? Do the same features trigger for similar information needs on out-of-distribution data?
Can you provide causal evidence (e.g., interventions on specific SAE features) that the detected features correspond to the “what” (entity/temporal/relation) rather than generic uncertainty or spurious correlates?
What is the run-time overhead of SAE encoding and cumulative-tracking per decoding step? Please provide latency and memory measurements compared to simple probability-based triggers and to no-retrieval baselines.
sections.overall_assessment:
Estimated Score:
                    4.1/10
                    (Calibrated to ICLR scale)
                
            The paper explores an appealing idea—using sparse, interpretable features to detect not only when but also what information to retrieve during multi-hop reasoning—and proposes a simple cumulative-activation gating mechanism with heuristic feature-to-query mapping. Unfortunately, the current experimental evidence is insufficient. All accuracy metrics are 0.00, core baselines are nonfunctional (IRCoT) or uninformative (Probing-RAG without effectiveness gains), and the evaluation uses synthetic data and a simulated LLM. Claims about interpretability and specificity of information needs are not convincingly validated; feature annotation and mapping are underspecified and lack rigorous assessment. While the direction is promising and could provide value if demonstrated on real tasks with solid baselines and causal/interpretability analysis, the current submission falls short of ICLR standards for empirical validation and methodological clarity. I recommend rejection at this stage, with encouragement to (1) run on established multi-hop benchmarks with real LLMs and functioning baselines, (2) provide cost–quality tradeoff analyses, (3) rigorously evaluate feature semantics and causal relevance, and (4) measure latency/overhead to substantiate “real-time” claims.
